insight: Lifespan does not tick down when a unit is exhausted, which is a known bug/quirk in the client. This causes issues with units that have both Exhaust and Lifespan, making it impossible to cleanly design units with that combination.
author: namington
context: Discussion about designing a unit with a 4-4-7 or 7-0-7 attack profile using Exhaust and Lifespan together, where the bug was identified as the blocker.

insight: Prismata enforces a strict resource conversion ordering (the 'conversion line') where certain conversions are only permitted one-way. Specifically, Attack can only be converted to Gold (not vice versa), and violating this ordering can create gamestates that are only reachable via Undo or even infinite resource loops, breaking game integrity.
author: namington
context: Discussion about a custom unit (Antiride Core) that paid Gold to gain Attack, which was identified as violating the conversion line rule that the developers enforce.

insight: The Simultaneous Decision Rule (SDR) requires that having more of a resource or unit is never strictly worse than having less. Golden Armor technically violates SDR in edge cases: for example, having golden Engineers is strictly worse than non-golden Engineers when facing a golden-armor-targeting ability like Lucina's, because the opponent benefits from targeting golden units.
author: mrguy888
context: Extended community discussion about SDR and which mechanics violate it, with mrguy888 providing the specific golden Lucina + Engineers example.

insight: Golden Armor was intentionally introduced despite its minor SDR violation because it ensures that when a unit is purchased it can be used at least once, which felt better from a player experience standpoint. It was tested against alternatives and chosen for feel. It is also critically important for Cluster Bolt gameplay.
author: mrguy888
context: Community discussion on the origin and justification for Golden Armor as a mechanic, with mrguy888 explaining the developer rationale.

insight: A 'Hivemind' mechanic (where clicking one unit of a type forces all units of that type to click simultaneously) only violates SDR if the click ability has an external resource cost (e.g., pay 1 Gold). If the only cost is sacrificing the unit itself, SDR is preserved because having more Hivemind units never makes the position strictly worse.
author: namington
context: Design theory discussion about a proposed Hivemind keyword and its SDR implications, with namington and http404error analyzing the edge cases.

insight: Draining opponent resources (Gold, Green) as a unit ability is a design area that inherently risks SDR violations, because punishing opponents for having resources or creating situations where having a resource is worse than not having it could violate the rule that more resources are never strictly worse.
author: swapgo
context: Discussion of a custom unit called Leech that drained opponent Gold and Green, where swapgo flagged the SDR implications of resource-draining mechanics.

insight: One unblocked point of attack typically destroys approximately 2 Gold worth of opponent resources, which is the baseline for evaluating whether disruption abilities (like resource draining) are more valuable than raw damage.
author: swapgo
context: Discussion about whether a resource-draining unit ability is worth more than dealing equivalent damage, used as a baseline comparison.

insight: Resource-draining effects are only strategically meaningful when they are more valuable than dealing equivalent damage, which typically occurs in the early game or in sets with strong soak options where direct damage is less efficient.
author: cachotognax
context: Discussion about the Leech custom unit's click ability that drained opponent Gold and Green, evaluating when such effects would be useful.

insight: Self-freeze effects (exhausting your own blockers as a purchase cost) are only meaningful as a drawback if the unit is purchased mid-game when the opponent already has attackers and you have defenders to freeze. If the unit can be rushed in the early game before either player has significant attack, the self-freeze is irrelevant and the unit becomes underpriced.
author: supergreyman
context: Design discussion about a custom unit Frosted Relay whose purchase cost exhausted friendly blockers, analyzing when the drawback actually matters.

insight: Tatsu Nullifier is considered a strong enough benchmark that a hypothetical vigilant unit providing 2 Attack per turn plus a free Frostbite per turn at a cheap cost would 'make Tatsu look bad,' suggesting Tatsu is a high-value reference point for evaluating vigilant attackers with secondary effects.
author: supergreyman
context: Design discussion about the Frosted Relay custom unit, with supergreyman arguing it was too efficient relative to Tatsu Nullifier.

insight: When a unit's card text constructs another unit without specifying build time, the default is the constructed unit's standard build time, not prompt. Real units like Frost Brooder specify build time explicitly for clarity, and designers should always explicitly state non-default build times on additional constructed effects to avoid ambiguity.
author: shadourow
context: Discussion about whether Frosted Relay's constructed Frostbites would inherit the standard BT2 or be prompt, with shadourow arguing build time should always be explicitly stated.

insight: Prismata draws are very rare despite the game being deterministic, because the game snowballs heavily—early advantages compound into insurmountable leads, making symmetrical endgame positions uncommon. Cluster Bolt sets are one of the few known cases where draws can occur, and Golden Armor is credited with preventing many of those draws.
author: consensus
context: Community discussion about why draws are uncommon in Prismata despite it being a perfect-information deterministic game, with multiple experts agreeing on the snowball dynamic.
